name: delete msg but keep socket id

label: Easy FE Updates
info:
This flow tests out the various HTML helpers
provided by uibuilder to make updating web
pages as simple and easy as possible.

## Special Attributes

* `uib-topic`
* `uib-var`
* `uib-bine` (EXPERIMENTAL)

## Custom web components (built-in)

* `<uib-var>` - Substitute dynamic data into 
  the UI (similar to {{varname}} in frameworks).

* `<apply-template>` - Apply the content of
  an HTML <template> to the UI without the need for JavaScript.

* `<uib-meta>` - Display page metadata such
  as the created/last-updated timestamp or size.
  Taken from the physical page file.

* `<uib-control>` - Change the styling and other
  parameters of the uibuilder client.

name: Setup - run this first to set up the page and style - only needs to run once
We will automatically send our data when a client connects. Load the page to match the numbers with the outputs
Tests the different easy ways to update web page \n content and styling using simple messages from Node-RED
Example updated: 2026-02-15 \n Tested using UIBUILDER v7.6.0
index.html
template:
Easy Update Testing

Using UIBUILDER for Node-RED

UIBUILDER-specific HTML attributes
Note that you can add these attributes to any HTML element.

This gives you a lot of flexibility in how you structure your page and where you want to data-driven content updates.

See the Reactivity - dynamic HTML attributes page in the documentation for more details of how these attributes work and the different options available.

1️⃣ uib-topic attribute (since v7.0.0)

This is by far the simplest method to update the content of an element on the page. Using the uib-topic attribute, we simply need to send a message from Node-RED with the matching topic name. It is likely to be the best choice for where you want to update the content from Node-RED.

The msg.payload will replace the content of the element. This can contain HTML. 

<div>
    <span uib-topic="set-by-simple-msg"></span>
</div>

2️⃣ uib-var attribute (since v7.6.0)

Using the uib-var attribute is marginally more complex than uib-topic, however it offers more flexibility. It is likely to be the best choice if dealing with updates in front-end JavaScript.

In this example, we use a remote command message from Node-RED to set the variable name and value. This means that we don't need to add any code to our index.js. But, the Node-RED message is more complex, e.g. msg._uib = {"command":"set","prop":"setByRemoteCmd","value":{"payload": "Hello from Node-RED"}}.

Note how the value property looks like a normal Node-RED message. You can also add an attributes property to set attributes on the element, e.g. msg.value.attributes = {class:"red-text", title:"This is a tooltip"}. 

<div>
    <span uib-var="setByRemoteCmd"></span>
</div>

3️⃣ uib-var attribute using JavaScript (since v7.6.0)

This example again uses the uib-var attribute, but this time we use JavaScript code in our index.js to update a managed variable "manually".

We also demonstrate using a deep property of an object variable instead of a simple variable name.

While this may seem a more complex approach, it is highly flexible. It gives you full control over the variable value and when to update it.

You can also use this approach to update the variable from other sources, e.g. user interactions on the page, without needing to send messages from Node-RED. There are many other possibilities.

In this case, we will still take a message from Node-RED but we will process it in our index.js to extract the value we want to set and then update the variable. 

<div>
    <span uib-var="setByJavaScript.deep.deeper"></span>
</div>

UIBUILDER built-in custom HTML web components
See the Built-in Web Components example flow for more examples of using the web components. Also see the Custom web components page in the documentation for details of how to use them and their extra attributes.

4️⃣ <uib-var topic="..."> (since v6.6.0)

Using the uib-var web component, we have a similar approach to using the custom attributes. This was the first "easy update" method added to UIBUILDER and is still supported, but the uib-topic and uib-var attributes are now the easier approaches for most use cases.

Use the same update approachs as for the uib-topic attribute above.

No type="html" is used in this example so the HTML in the payload is ignored. 

<div>
    <uib-var topic="set-by-simple-msg"></uib-var>
</div>

5️⃣ <uib-var variable="..."> (since v6.6.0)

Using the uib-var web component, we have a similar approach to using the custom attributes. This was the first "easy update" method added to UIBUILDER and is still supported, but the uib-topic and uib-var attributes are now the easier approaches for most use cases.

Use the same update approachs as for the uib-var attribute above. 

<div>
    <uib-var variable="setByJavaScript2" type="object"></uib-var>
</div>

Differences between the attributes and the web component

The main differences between the custom attributes and the web components are:

  • The custom attributes are simpler and easier to use for basic updates.
  • The custom attributes can be placed on any HTML element.
  • The custom attributes rely on the browser monitoring changes to the DOM. While this is very efficient, it does mean that if you create an extremely large page with lots of these attributes, and especially if you are pushing data to them very rapidly, then you might start to see the browser struggling to keep up. In contrast, the web components are more self-contained and so may perform better in these extreme cases. Note, however, that you would need to have a very large page and be pushing data very rapidly to see any difference in performance. For most use cases, the custom attributes will be perfectly fine.
  • The web components support some extra attributes: filter, undefined, report, and type which allow a lot more control over the formatting and behavior.
  • The custom attributes will treat the content as HTML and so will render it as such. They even will use embeded <style> and run embedded <script> code. You should therefore take care to sanitize any HTML inputs.
  • The web components content payload is assumed to be plain text by default. To allow HTML content, you need to set the type attribute to "html". For complex objects, you can set the type to "object" and then the content will be treated as a JavaScript object and shown as a syntax-highlighted JSON string. The custom attributes will treat the content as HTML and so will render it as such, which is simpler but less flexible.

See the documentation for details: Easy UI Updates.

This page and its accompanying Node-RED flow are designed to test the ease of updating web pages from simple Node-RED messages.

We will start with the simplest method but also show others that may be helpful in different circumstances. The goal is to demonstrate how quickly and easily you can update the content of a web page using UIBUILDER and Node-RED, without needing to write complex JavaScript or HTML code or even much of a Node-RED flow.

If you examine the HTML source, note that nearly all of the code is explanation. There are only a few lines that are actually doing anything.
The data-driven updates on this page are triggered in Node-RED simply by loading this page.
Note that updates do not need to come from Node-RED!

They can come from your own JavaScript code or from user interactions on the page, e.g. button clicks, form submissions, etc.

These features provide the equivalent of "reactivity" features in modern JavaScript frameworks, but without needing to write any complex code or load a large, complex framework. You can have a fully reactive page with just a few lines of code and simple Node-RED messages.

rules.to: {"command":"set","prop":"setByRemoteCmd","value":{"payload":"This was set by a remote command that updated a uibuilder managed variable setByRemoteCmd.","attributes":{"style":"color: hsl(270, 50%, 90%);","title":"We set this from Node-RED as well!"}}}
This also set this from Node-RED but processed in the front-end
